Q: Shifted from treating Quant as formula execution to treating it as strategic problem solving.
Accepted that strong math background did not automatically translate to GMAT Quant success.
Took about 30 seconds before solving to identify what the question was really testing and where the shortcut might be.
Built a consistent framework: read carefully, extract inferences, consider edge cases, solve, and verify that the answer made sense.
Reviewed solution videos to identify the trick or inference missed, not merely to see the answer.
Used diagnostic quizzes to locate strong and weak topics, then watched targeted lessons and did targeted practice.
overall: Raised Quant from Q79 to Q87 through a mental shift rather than a purely technical one.
Used organized prep materials with strong explanations and mixed official practice with regular practice.
Practiced under timed conditions with sectional mocks.
Trained with harder-than-test questions so official Quant felt more manageable.
Trusted the practiced process on test day even when easier late-section questions created doubt.

Q: Treated Quant as solid and focused mainly on minimizing silly mistakes.
V: Improved Verbal by changing the approach to questions rather than adding new grammar or vocabulary content.
For RC, stopped skimming to save time and instead engaged with the passage upfront.
Used both untimed and timed quizzes to make the new Verbal strategies consistent under pressure.
Read MSR-style passages in one go to absorb detail across sections when possible.
DI: Practiced MSR strategies and time management before the final attempt.
Entered DI with the attitude that the problems were solvable, which helped performance in mocks.
Accepted that DI did not need to be perfect if Verbal and Quant were strong enough.
Recognized that test-day DI could underperform mock DI and still leave room for a 700+ score.
overall: After scoring 685 twice despite 700+ mocks, focused on execution and mental approach rather than months of additional content.
Went into the third attempt with fewer expectations and less attachment to the result.
Prepared from April to late September using e-GMAT plus GMAT Club and GMAT Ninja videos.
Viewed inconsistent practice and missed goals as normal rather than proof that progress was not real.

Q: Prevented Quant overconfidence by solving every question fully even when the answer seemed obvious.
Maintained a detailed error log and reviewed it regularly to identify mistake patterns.
Used extra Quant time on the second attempt to catch and correct a mistake.
Targeted algebra specifically after mentor-guided skill data showed it as a weakness.
Practiced enough to improve speed while preserving accuracy.
V: Used CR pre-thinking and stuck with it once practice showed it worked.
Practiced predicting the correct CR answer before looking at choices to improve accuracy and confidence.
For RC, broke down passages and identified main idea and purpose quickly.
Used GMAT Ninja videos to improve RC structure work.
Practiced Verbal extensively in Scholaranium to track progress and weak areas.
DI: Experimented with skipping MSR and returning later, but abandoned it when it did not improve results.
Settled on answering DI in sequence while assuming that two questions could be guessed if needed.
Practiced to improve speed and accuracy across all DI question types.
Treated DI as an adaptive strategy problem: adjust to what actually works rather than forcing an initial plan.
overall: Used a structured study plan to reduce decision fatigue during preparation.
Balanced GMAT prep with a full-time job and relied on mentor accountability.
Analyzed mocks for time management and decision-making, not just wrong answers.
Experimented with section order and chose Verbal, Quant, then DI.
Built a test-day routine with an early wake-up, a short run, and early arrival at the center.

Q: Used topic-wise practice rather than random questions to recognize repeated Quant patterns.
Tracked weak areas granularly, such as translating word problems into equations, instead of labeling all Quant as weak.
Built familiarity with variations of rate, work, linear equations, number properties, and LCM/HCF questions.
Used repetition to reduce the mental bandwidth spent just understanding what the question asked.
V: For CR, identified the main conclusion first and then examined how the premises supported it.
Before checking answer choices, paused to understand what kind of answer was needed.
When stuck between choices, returned to the exact wording of the question and eliminated the option that did not answer it precisely.
For RC, slowed down on the first read to avoid inefficient rereading.
Paid special attention to transition words and author opinions because they often drive main point and tone questions.
overall: Improved from 575 to 675 over about three months of focused effort.
Used e-GMAT throughout the prep.
Changed approach rather than simply studying harder.
Treated each timed question as an opportunity-cost decision and moved on after about 2.5 minutes if not close.
Took mocks at the same time of day as the actual exam to train focus and stamina.
Did not study the night before the test, prioritizing rest and execution.

Q: Used tough Quant practice to build resilience beyond an already strong base.
Solved about 700 Quant questions across topics.
Used mock and platform analytics to track accuracy by topic.
Maintained composure after an early Quant mistake on test day and finished strong.
V: Targeted RC after realizing prior preparation was insufficient for complex official passages.
Used CR pre-thinking and visualization to break down arguments instead of guessing.
Practiced answering RC questions without the passage visible to force true comprehension.
Solved about 600 CR and RC questions from Scholaranium, Verbal Online, GMAT Club, OG, and Official Advanced Questions.
Used hard-question accuracy stats to verify improvement before test day.
DI: Focused on speed reading and equation formation for Data Sufficiency.
Avoided mental calculations in DS where written structure was safer.
Practiced about 500 DI questions, mostly from Scholaranium.
Used cementing quizzes and topic tests to refine specific DI subareas.
Prioritized questions and managed time deliberately under DI pressure.
overall: Moved from 635 and 655 official attempts to 715 after switching to a more guided plan.
Used a personalized last-mile study plan to target weaknesses and preserve strengths.
After each mock, kept a detailed spreadsheet of mistakes and strategies for accuracy and timing.
Used targeted practice between mocks rather than treating mocks as isolated events.
Followed a practiced order of Quant, Verbal, break, then DI and stayed calm through the final section.

Q: Used concept files to fill Quant gaps after starting with an engineering background but uneven execution.
Completed roughly 750 Quant questions and practiced consistently to stay sharp.
Analyzed mistakes for patterns, especially in time-speed-distance, work-rate, divisibility, even-odd, and careless errors.
Marked difficult Quant questions and returned when time allowed.
Reframed unexpectedly hard Quant questions as evidence of higher scoring potential rather than a reason to panic.
V: Focused heavily on CR after starting from a weak Verbal baseline.
Used pre-thinking before answer choices for weaken, assumption, and strengthen questions.
Studied both correct and incorrect answer explanations in detail.
Accepted that pre-thinking was initially slow but would become faster with practice.
Improved boldface questions by categorizing each statement’s role and tracking relationships between argument parts.
DI: Moved DI to the middle of the section order to attempt it with more energy.
Completed a broad DI question bank covering most question types.
Prioritized the first five to six DI questions, then increased pace.
Identified complex Two-Part Analysis questions with multiple conditions as potential skips.
Learned that leaving DI questions blank had caused a severe diagnostic penalty and made completion a priority.
overall: Improved from a 455 diagnostic to 695 over three attempts by changing both strategy and mindset.
Used one primary platform consistently rather than jumping among many methods.
Spent at least as much time analyzing each mock as taking it, re-solving wrong questions before checking answers.
Used a four-month break after the second attempt to avoid burnout.
Prepared physically and mentally for test day with light final days, a test-center practice drive, sleep, food control, and a repeatable routine.
